cataloging_subject.label: The Destruction of Troy
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Count one intellectual work once. The prologue and thirty-six books are 37 representation sections of that work, not 37 works and not 37 count-bearing content parts.

catalog_summary.title: The Destruction of Troy
catalog_summary.author: John Clerk of Whalley (attributed)
catalog_summary.editor: George A. Panton and David Donaldson
catalog_summary.date: Second half of the fourteenth century (MEC: ?a1400)

identifiers.scheme: Manual of the Writings in Middle English

titles.value: Gest Historiale of the Destruction of Troy
The Gest Hystoriale of the Destruction of Troy

assertion.notes: Edition-associated title retained as a source-specific variant, not preferred as the abstract work title.

agents.name: John Clerk of Whalley
notes: Current responsibility is attributed author at medium confidence; a separate translator or adapter role remains open.

agents.name: George A. Panton
notes: Source-edition coeditor for Part I; not asserted as a creator of the medieval work.

agents.name: David Donaldson
notes: Source-edition editor across the Panton-Donaldson aggregate; no separate transcriber responsibility is projected because the inspected title/header evidence says edited, not transcribed.

assertion.notes: Glasgow explicitly catalogs the work under John Clerk of Whalley. The public Turville-Petre landing page confirms the article title and indexed John Clerk/authorship/Whalley topics but exposes no abstract or argument; an additional translator or adapter role therefore remains open.

scope.description: Panton-Donaldson source-edition Part I: target lines 1-8884, printed pages 1-288, TIFFs 75-364, EETS Original Series 39 (1869).
Panton-Donaldson source-edition representation: coeditor of Part I and editor of Part II, covering target lines 1-14044, printed pages 1-459, and TIFFs 75-535.

notes: No separate transcriber role is projected from the currently inspected evidence.

date_statements.display: Second half of the fourteenth century

assertion.notes: MEC's questioned ante-1400 value is retained alongside, not merged into, DMER's 1350-1399 range.

notes: Nuthurst provenance is ownership history, not evidence for composition or copying location.

form_statements.description: Unrhymed alliterative verse; DMER describes an AA/AX pattern with variations.

summaries.value: A single Middle English alliterative romance translating and adapting Guido delle Colonne's Historia destructionis Troiae, surviving in the unique known manuscript copy Glasgow MS Hunter 388 and represented here by a prologue and thirty-six books.

The Panton-Donaldson edition's front matter, manuscript index, notes, glossary, advertisements, and blank page 460 are outside the cataloged work boundary. The prologue and thirty-six books are 37 representation sections of one work.

extent.description: One verse work represented by a prologue and thirty-six numbered books: 37 representation sections, 249 encoded LG verse groups, and 14,044 encoded L elements. The section count does not create 37 works or 37 content items.

content_parts.label: The Destruction of Troy
content_parts.summary: The single cataloged Middle English alliterative Troy romance, represented by a prologue and thirty-six books.

locators.value: BODY DIV1 positions 2-38 inclusive (generated NODE suffixes 5-41)

notes: The prologue and Books 1-36 are representation sections within this one content part; they are not independently counted works.

related_works.title: Historia destructionis Troiae
notes: The relationship is a medieval translation-adaptation rather than a claim of close continuous translation.
No direct lineage relation to Benoît de Sainte-Maure's Roman de Troie is asserted without additional reviewed evidence.

evidence.summary: The exact XML header supplies the U-M identifier, language, edition description, OCR/TEI Level-1 production statement, legacy availability warning, and image-linkage statement.

evidence.citation: Panton-Donaldson title pages in the complete U-M raw package.
evidence.summary: The inspected title pages identify the two EETS parts, their publication dates, and Panton and Donaldson's editorial responsibilities.
evidence.locator: Part I and Part II title pages

evidence.citation: APE7380 target-boundary, page, and folio audit.
evidence.summary: The durable machine audit establishes the one-work boundary, 37 representation sections, 249 verse groups, 14,044 lines, printed-page and TIFF ranges, edition-part boundary, and folio milestones.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance record for Gest Historiale of the Destruction of Troy.
evidence.summary: The institutional scholarly record supports the work title variant, second-half-fourteenth-century date, Lancashire localization, alliterative verse and romance classification, and Guido source relationship.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance manuscript record for Glasgow, MS Hunter 388.
evidence.summary: The record supports the unique Hunter 388 witness, its c.1538-46 copy date, and the work's folios 2r-214v.
BIB853 supports the preferred short title The Destruction of Troy.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium Bibliography BIB853 identifiers.
evidence.summary: BIB853 supplies IMEV 2129, NIMEV 2129, DIMEV 3443, and Manual of the Writings in Middle English 1.I.72.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium Bibliography BIB853 hand records.
evidence.summary: The record preserves two LALME hands, LP 411 and LP 23, both localized to Lancashire.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium Bibliography BIB853 editions.
evidence.summary: The record identifies the Panton-Donaldson source edition and Matsumoto's 2002 diplomatic/color-facsimile edition.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium Bibliography BIB853 date stencils.
evidence.summary: The record preserves the separate c1540 manuscript date and questioned ?a1400 work date.

evidence.citation: University of Glasgow catalog record for MS Hunter 388.
evidence.summary: The current catalog supports the shelfmark, John Clerk attribution, translated-from-Guido description, prior Huchown attribution history, historical one-scribe/two-styles description, and zero-canvas access state.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan CME item APE7380.0001.001.
evidence.summary: The current item record supports the source-edition title and publication, Guido source attribution, Troy subject, and current item identity.

evidence.citation: Thorlac Turville-Petre, ‘The Author of The Destruction of Troy,’ Medium Ævum 57.2 (1988), p. 264, public landing page.
evidence.summary: The landing page establishes the article's bibliographic identity and indexed John Clerk, authorship, translation, Whalley, Lancashire, Whalley Abbey, acrostic, and alliteration topics; it exposes no abstract or argument text.
notes: Do not treat indexed topics as proof of the article's argument or conclusion details.

open_questions.question: Should John Clerk of Whalley receive an additional translator and/or adapter responsibility alongside the attributed-author responsibility?
next_steps: Review Turville-Petre's full article or comparably direct scholarship before adding a distinct translator or adapter role.
Keep the work-level Guido translation-adaptation relation separate from personal responsibility vocabulary.

open_questions.question: How should DMER's second-half-fourteenth-century date and MEC's questioned ?a1400 value ultimately be normalized?
next_steps: Review a direct scholarly dating argument; until then retain both source-attributed assertions.

open_questions.question: How should MEC/LALME's two hands and Glasgow's historical one-scribe/two-styles description be reconciled?
next_steps: Consult a modern codicological study; until then preserve the claims separately with their source attribution.

open_questions.question: What access and reuse terms govern the 2002 Matsumoto diplomatic and color-facsimile CD-ROM?
next_steps: Obtain a publisher or rights-holder statement; holdings, publisher availability, retailer access, and reuse remain separate facts.

notes: Count one intellectual work once. The prologue and thirty-six books are 37 representation sections, not 37 works or 37 count-bearing content parts.
The target is BODY DIV1 positions 2-38 (generated NODE suffixes 5-41), printed pages 1-459 including inserted pages 12* and 13*, TIFFs 75-535, and witness folios 2r-214v.
Exclude all front matter, errata, the manuscript index, blank printed page 460, notes from page 461, glossary, advertisements, and all back matter from the cataloged-work boundary.
John Clerk is an attributed author at medium confidence. The prior Sir Hugh of Eglinton/Huchown attribution is catalog history, not a current responsibility.
The Turville-Petre landing page supports bibliographic identity and indexed topics only, not the article's argument details.
